skill_name: SQL Injection and Unsafe Query Construction Review
trigger: Use when a PR modifies database access, raw SQL, query construction, search/filter/login endpoints, or user-controlled values in queries.
risk_profile: Missing parameter binding for user-controlled values
Escaping or string formatting mistaken for parameterization
Sensitive data exposure through overly broad query behavior
Query construction that changes semantics for special characters
context_plan: Inspect changed database access code and identify all user-controlled values that reach a query
Compare unsafe string construction with existing parameterized query patterns in the repository
Check whether the suggested fix binds every dynamic value through placeholders or query builder parameters
Look for search, filter, login, and lookup paths where attacker-controlled input can affect SQL structure
checklist: Does any SQL string interpolate request, route, form, or function parameters directly?
Are placeholders paired with complete argument arrays or query-builder bindings?
Does the fix avoid manual escaping as the primary defense?
Could wildcard, quote, comment, or boolean payloads alter query semantics?
Are tests proposed for malicious input and normal lookup behavior?
test_templates: A lookup containing quotes or SQL comment syntax is treated as data and does not alter the query structure
The repository database mock verifies that user input is passed as a bound parameter
Normal search/filter behavior still returns expected records after parameterization

trigger: Use when a PR modifies HTML rendering, template output, user-generated content display, or DOM manipulation with user input.
risk_profile: Cross-site scripting through unsanitized user input in HTML
DOM-based XSS through unsafe innerHTML or dangerouslySetInnerHTML
Missing output encoding for user-controlled data
Stored XSS through persisted user content
context_plan: Identify all user-controlled values that reach HTML output or DOM manipulation
Check for proper output encoding or sanitization before rendering
Verify template engines auto-escape by default or explicit escaping is used
Look for innerHTML, dangerouslySetInnerHTML, or equivalent unsafe DOM APIs
checklist: Is user input properly escaped before HTML rendering?
Are template engines configured for auto-escaping?
Is innerHTML or dangerouslySetInnerHTML used with user input?
Are URL parameters, form inputs, and stored content sanitized?
Are tests proposed for script injection attempts?
test_templates: User input containing <script> tags is escaped and rendered as text
Event handler attributes in user input are neutralized
Stored user content with malicious payloads does not execute on display

trigger: Use when a PR modifies admin or privileged routes, authentication logic, session handling, JWT validation, role checks, or middleware ordering.
risk_profile: Missing authorization checks for admin/privileged operations
Privilege escalation through inconsistent role checks
JWT validation bypass or weak secret handling
context_plan: Inspect route handlers for authentication and authorization checks
Read router mounting and middleware configuration files
Compare with established auth patterns in the repository
Check middleware order and alternate mount paths
Review session and token validation logic
checklist: Is authentication enforced before privileged handlers run?
Is role-based authorization checked after authentication?
Could route mounting or middleware order expose unprotected paths?
Are session tokens validated and regenerated properly?
Are JWT secrets strong and validation complete?
Are tests present for unauthenticated and unauthorized access?
test_templates: Unauthenticated requests to privileged endpoints return 401
Authenticated non-admin requests to admin endpoints return 403
Authenticated admin requests succeed
Invalid or expired tokens are rejected
Session fixation attacks are prevented

trigger: Use when a PR modifies request handling, form processing, API endpoints, file uploads, or user input validation.
risk_profile: Missing input validation allows malformed data
Path traversal through unsanitized file paths
Command injection through shell execution with user input
context_plan: Identify all user input entry points (params, body, headers, files)
Check for validation schemas or explicit validation logic
Look for file path construction with user input
Inspect shell command execution with user-controlled values
Review URL construction and HTTP client usage
checklist: Are all user inputs validated against expected types and formats?
Are file paths sanitized to prevent directory traversal?
Is shell command execution avoided or properly escaped?
Are URLs validated before making outbound requests?
Are object properties whitelisted to prevent mass assignment?
Are tests proposed for malicious input patterns?
test_templates: Invalid input types are rejected with appropriate error messages
Path traversal attempts (../, absolute paths) are blocked
Command injection payloads are neutralized or rejected
SSRF attempts to internal URLs are prevented
Mass assignment of protected fields is blocked

skill_name: Error Handling and Information Disclosure Review
trigger: Use when a PR modifies error handling, exception catching, logging, error responses, or async control flow.
risk_profile: Swallowed errors leading to silent failures
Information disclosure through verbose error messages
context_plan: Trace error handling from try/catch blocks to response emission
Check promise chains for proper rejection handling
Review error messages for sensitive information disclosure
Verify async operations are awaited before success responses
Inspect error logging and monitoring integration
checklist: Are all errors properly caught and handled?
Are promise rejections handled or propagated?
Do error responses avoid exposing sensitive details?
Are stack traces hidden from production responses?
Is async work completed before success is sent?
Are errors logged for debugging without exposing to clients?
Are tests present for error scenarios?
test_templates: Thrown errors return appropriate HTTP error codes
Rejected promises are caught and handled
Error responses contain generic messages without stack traces
Async failures prevent false success responses
Error logs capture details without exposing to clients

trigger: Use when a PR modifies caching logic, cache keys, invalidation, mutation paths, or cached data access.
risk_profile: Cache key mismatch between reads and writes
context_plan: Trace cache key construction in read and write paths
Verify invalidation happens after successful mutations
Check for cache key consistency across operations
Review cache TTL and eviction policies
Look for race conditions in cache updates
checklist: Do update paths invalidate the same keys used by reads?
Are cache keys consistent across create, read, update, delete?
Can reads return stale data after updates?
Are race conditions in cache updates handled?
Is cache poisoning prevented through validation?
Are tests present for cache coherence?
test_templates: Read after update returns fresh data, not stale cache
Cache keys match exactly between read and invalidation
Concurrent updates maintain cache consistency
Invalid data cannot poison the cache

trigger: Use when a PR modifies database queries, loops, API calls, file operations, or resource-intensive operations.
context_plan: Identify database queries in loops
Check for pagination on list endpoints
Review resource cleanup (connections, files, streams)
Look for algorithmic inefficiencies
Inspect rate limiting and throttling mechanisms
checklist: Are database queries batched instead of looped?
Is pagination implemented for large result sets?
Are resources (connections, files) properly closed?
Are algorithms efficient for expected data sizes?
Is rate limiting present on public endpoints?
Are tests present for performance scenarios?
test_templates: List endpoints with many records execute in constant queries
Large datasets are paginated and don't cause memory issues
Resources are released after use
Rate limiting prevents abuse

trigger: Use when a PR modifies encryption, hashing, password handling, API keys, tokens, or secrets management.
risk_profile: Secrets logged or exposed in errors
context_plan: Check for hardcoded secrets in code
Review cryptographic algorithm choices
Verify password hashing uses bcrypt/argon2/scrypt
Inspect random number generation for security-sensitive operations
Look for secrets in logs or error messages
checklist: Are secrets loaded from environment variables or secret managers?
Are modern, secure cryptographic algorithms used?
Are passwords hashed with bcrypt, argon2, or scrypt?
Is sensitive data encrypted at rest and in transit?
Is cryptographically secure random generation used?
Are secrets excluded from logs and error messages?
Are tests present without exposing real secrets?
test_templates: Passwords are hashed with appropriate work factors
Secrets are not present in code or version control
Encryption uses modern algorithms (AES-256, etc.)
Random tokens are cryptographically secure

trigger: Use for all PRs to generate comprehensive test coverage including security, edge cases, and regression tests.
risk_profile: Untested edge cases and error paths
No regression tests for bug fixes
context_plan: Analyze changed code to identify testable behaviors
Generate security tests for identified vulnerabilities
Create edge case tests for boundary conditions
Write regression tests for bug fixes
Include negative-path tests for error handling
checklist: Are security tests present for all identified risks?
Are edge cases and boundary conditions tested?
Are error paths and exception handling tested?
Are integration tests present for multi-component changes?
Do tests cover both positive and negative scenarios?
Are tests deterministic and not flaky?
test_templates: Security tests verify protection against identified attack vectors
Edge case tests cover boundary conditions (empty, null, max values)
Error tests verify proper handling of failures
Integration tests verify component interactions
Regression tests prevent reintroduction of fixed bugs

skill_name: Low-Context PR Triage and Repository Validation
trigger: Use when PR review input lacks a real diff, changed-file context, repository metadata, title, description, or uses placeholder/mock content.
risk_profile: Placeholder diff can cause speculative findings
Missing changed files blocks impact analysis
Missing PR metadata obscures intended behavior
Repository extraction failures can create misleading review conditions
context_plan: Validate whether the diff is real and the changed files are accessible
Request PR title, description, changed files, and file contents when missing
Avoid file-specific findings until concrete source evidence is available
Defer correctness, security, performance, and test assessment until source context exists
checklist: Is there a real unified diff with changed files?
Are changed files accessible in the repository?
Is PR intent described by title, description, or equivalent metadata?
Are review limitations explicitly stated instead of inventing findings?
Is the minimum missing context requested?
test_templates: Placeholder or empty diffs produce triage-only review output
Missing file context suppresses code-specific findings
Repository access failures are surfaced as process blockers
